mnemonics.title: Zero Trust Principles
mnemonics.content: ZTV - Zero Trust: Never trust, always verify; Verify explicitly; Assume breach; Least privilege access

mnemonics.title: OAuth Flow
mnemonics.content: RARA - Request authorization, User approves, Return code, Access token issued

acronyms.usage: Framework for managing digital identities and access rights
System that authenticates users and provides identity information
Authentication scheme allowing one login for multiple applications
Using multiple verification methods for identity confirmation
Controlling and auditing elevated access to critical systems
Compact token format for secure claim transmission
Identity layer on OAuth 2.0 for authentication
Kerberos ticket for requesting service tickets

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a hotel key card system: User (guest) asks front desk (authorization server) for access → Hotel gives key card (token) → User presents key to room (resource server) → Room grants access
Think of a passport: Header = visa type (algorithm info), Payload = personal details (claims), Signature = embassy stamp (verification seal)
Imagine airport security: Even if you have a boarding pass (previous authentication), you must still go through security check (continuous verification) at every gate (every resource access)
Like a concert VIP system: Ticket office (KDC) gives you wristband (TGT) → Show wristband to get backstage pass (service ticket) → Access specific areas
Think of a corporate phonebook: Organized tree structure, each entry has attributes (name, department, phone), queried using specific addresses (DNs)

analogies.analogy: Authentication is showing your ID at the door (proving who you are), Authorization is the VIP pass you receive that says which areas you can access.
Like a valet key for your car - it gives limited access (can't open trunk) without giving full access to all your keys.
Like a government issuing passports - one trusted source that other countries (service providers) accept for identity verification.
Like adding unique ingredients to the same recipe - even if two people use the same base ingredients (passwords), the final dishes (hashes) taste completely different.
Like a bank vault: Even after entering the building (network), every safe (resource) requires its own combination (verification) - no one has automatic access everywhere.
